county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Named for John Sutter, whose New Helvetia settlement preceded the Gold Rush. Yuba City is county seat. Sikh community (one of the oldest in the US). Agricultural county in the Sacramento Valley.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sutter County Recorder in Yuba City has records from 1850. Named for John Sutter, whose New Helvetia settlement was the first non-Native community in California's Central Valley. The Community Memorial Museum of Sutter County has local records. Sutter County has one of the oldest Sikh communities in America (Punjabi immigrants from early 1900s). FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Yuba City Appeal-Democrat newspaper provides obituaries. Agricultural census records are valuable for tracing farming families. Check California State Archives for Sutter's personal papers and land grant records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Yuba City — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1856 from Butte, Colusa, and Shasta counties. Sacramento Valley county. Red Bluff is county seat. Ishi, the last known member of the Yahi people, emerged near Tehama County in 1911. Ranching and agricultural economy.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tehama County Recorder in Red Bluff has deeds from 1856. Pre-1856 records split among Butte, Colusa, and Shasta counties. Tehama County Museum in Red Bluff preserves local history. Ishi, the last known member of the Yahi people, emerged near Deer Creek in 1911 — University of California records document this significant event. The Red Bluff Daily News newspaper provides obituaries. FamilySearch has some microfilmed county records. Check BLM land patents for homesteaders. Ranching families along the Sacramento River are well documented in assessment and brand registration records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Red Bluff — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Remote mountain county with limited population. Weaverville is county seat. Weaverville Joss House (1874) is California's oldest continuously used Chinese temple. Gold mining history.
county_metadata.research_tips: Trinity County Recorder in Weaverville has records from 1850. Despite being an original 1850 county, Trinity's remote mountain location means smaller populations and more manageable record sets. The Weaverville Joss House (1874) is California's oldest continuously used Chinese temple — Jake Jackson Memorial Museum adjacent preserves Gold Rush and Chinese community records. Mining claim records at BLM trace early settlers. FamilySearch has limited microfilmed records. The Trinity Journal newspaper (from 1856) provides obituaries. Hupa and other tribal records through NARA and Hoopa Valley Tribe.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Weaverville — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1852 from Mariposa County. Named for Tulare Lake (once the largest freshwater lake west of the Mississippi, now drained for agriculture). Sequoia National Park. Significant Yokuts tribal heritage. Agricultural powerhouse.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tulare County Recorder in Visalia has deeds from 1852. Pre-1852 records in Mariposa County. Tulare County Museum has local history holdings. Named for Tulare Lake, once the largest freshwater lake west of the Mississippi, now drained for agriculture. Yokuts tribal heritage records through NARA. Sequoia National Park records at National Park Service. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records extensively. The Visalia Times-Delta newspaper provides obituaries. Agricultural census records are essential for tracing farming families. Significant Japanese farming community before WWII internment.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Visalia — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Named for a Miwok village. Sonora was a rich Gold Rush camp. Columbia State Historic Park preserves a Gold Rush town. Hetch Hetchy Valley controversy. Miwok and Paiute tribal heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: Tuolumne County Recorder in Sonora has records from 1850. Columbia State Historic Park preserves a Gold Rush town — park archives have community records. Tuolumne County Museum and History Center in Sonora has Gold Rush-era records and photographs. Named for the Miwok village of Tuolumne. Mining claim records at BLM are critical. Hetch Hetchy Valley controversy generated extensive government records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Tuolumne Courier newspaper (from 1854) provides early obituaries. Miwok tribal records through NARA. Italian and Chilean miners well documented in local histories.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Sonora — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1872 from Santa Barbara County. Mission San Buenaventura (1782) was last mission founded by Father Serra. Oil industry from 1860s. Chumash tribal heritage. Agricultural and coastal community.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ventura County Recorder has deeds from 1872. Pre-1872 records in Santa Barbara County. Mission San Buenaventura (1782) records — the last mission founded by Father Serra — at Archdiocese of Los Angeles. Museum of Ventura County has extensive local collections. Oil industry records trace families from the 1860s onward (Ojai, Ventura, Santa Paula). Chumash tribal records through NARA and tribal governments. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Ventura Free Press and Ventura County Star newspapers provide obituaries. Rancho-era land grants are essential for early families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Named for the Patwin village of Yoloy. Agricultural county. University of California, Davis (1905). Woodland is county seat. Significant Japanese farming communities before WWII internment.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yolo County Recorder in Woodland has records from 1850. University of California, Davis Special Collections has agricultural and regional history. Named for the Patwin village of Yoloy. Yolo County Archives has indexed local records. Japanese farming communities thrived before WWII internment — check NARA records. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Daily Democrat newspaper (Woodland) provides obituaries. California State Agricultural Society records at California State Archives trace farm families. Check county school census records for children and families not captured in federal census.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Woodland — index and images vary by era

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Original 1850 county. Named for the Maidu village of Yubu. Marysville was a major Gold Rush supply town and river port. Significant Chinese community. 1862 Great Flood devastated the county. Hydraulic mining history.
county_metadata.research_tips: Yuba County Recorder in Marysville has records from 1850. Marysville was a major Gold Rush supply town and river port — its Chinese community was one of the largest in California. The 1862 Great Flood devastated Marysville and may have damaged some early records. Yuba County Library has local history holdings. Community Memorial Museum in Yuba City (Sutter County) covers both counties. Hydraulic mining records trace families in the gold fields. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The Marysville Appeal newspaper provides early obituaries. BLM mining claim records are essential for Gold Rush families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: County Recorder, Marysville — index and images vary by era
